topicArea: Manners, the Hypocrisy Charge & Codes That Protect and Price Out

questions.passage:
The guest reaches for the wrong fork, and nothing happens. No one speaks, no one stares, the conversation proceeds, and yet the error has been registered at the table with the completeness of a seismograph recording a distant tremor. There will be no consequence except that there already has been one. This is the peculiar jurisdiction of manners: a code no legislature enacts, no court enforces, and no citizen escapes, administered entirely through noticing, its verdicts delivered in glances and its sentences served in reputations.

What are the small rituals for? The unglamorous answer is coordination. Strangers who cannot know each other's hearts can know the forms, and the forms make encounters predictable: who greets first, how long to hold a hand, what to say when there is nothing to say. A grander tradition adds a developmental claim. The long history of table codes, the fork itself a late and once-ridiculed innovation, has been read as the slow schooling of impulse, each rule a small rehearsal of restraint, until the codes of the table become, in aggregate, a civilization's daily practice of considering others. On this account manners are morality's small change: minor denominations, but the currency in which ordinary regard is actually paid.

The charge sheet against them is equally old. Manners, the critics say, are a veneer that substance never signed: the practiced cad never misuses a fork, and polished cruelty is crueler for the polish. Cultures that prize sincerity accuse politeness cultures of institutionalized dishonesty, of preferring smooth falsehood to honest friction, and every adolescent who has been told to say sorry like you mean it has met the problem early: a form can be performed while its supposed content is absent, and the performance can even be the perfect disguise.

There is a darker count on the indictment. Codes must be learned, and unequal access to the learning converts etiquette into a gate. The rules that matter most are precisely the ones never written down where outsiders could study them, and fluency functions as a lineage certificate: the wrong fork, the wrong vowel, the thank-you note absent or excessive, each betrays the newcomer at the border of a class that pretends to have no borders. Etiquette manuals sell the passwords, but the passwords are changed once too many hold them, and the tolerated rudeness of the powerful, beside the punished rudeness of the weak, exposes how unevenly the code's penalties fall.

And yet the case for surface deserves a fuller hearing than sincerity cultures give it. Warmth cannot be commanded, but its forms can, and a society that waits for genuine feeling before extending courtesy will extend very little of it. The forms, moreover, protect in the direction sincerity does not: rituals of address and turn-taking restrain the confident and carve standing for the hesitant, and when an office abolishes its formalities in the name of authenticity, what rushes into the vacuum is rarely honesty; it is the advantage of whoever is loudest. Politeness, on this defense, is mutual armor, and the alternative to formality is not warmth but exposure.

The codes, meanwhile, refuse to hold still. Who rises, who pays, who may use first names, how quickly a message must be answered, whether a call without warning is a courtesy or an invasion: each generation is accused of rudeness by its elders while drafting, invisibly, the etiquette its children will be scolded with. The content turns over; the coding never stops, and the very people who declare manners dead are usually enforcing a new set, unaware that their casualness has its own forks, its own unforgivable errors, and its own quiet seismographs at every table.

The watchers at the table, then, were doing something real, reading a stranger's legibility to the group, and the reading was neither empty snobbery nor moral insight but the operation of a working agreement about friction. Manners are not morality, as the polished cad proves, and not its opposite, as the sneering sincerity-monger assumes. They are a technology of the surface, and the right question for any code is the one the indictment and the defense together teach: whom does this set of forms protect, and whom does it price out?
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because the practiced cad never misuses a fork, manners are a veneer that conceals character and should give way to sincerity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Table codes historically schooled impulse so successfully that modern societies no longer require formal etiquette.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Etiquette functions primarily as a class gate, and its unwritten rules exist chiefly to expose newcomers at social borders that pretend not to exist.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Manners are a surface technology, neither morality nor its mask, and each code should be judged by whom it shields and whom it prices out.

explanations.A: This choice adopts the third paragraph's indictment as the verdict, but the fifth paragraph answers it: warmth cannot be commanded while its forms can, and abolished formality yields not honesty but the advantage of the loudest. The cad proves manners are not morality, not that they should yield to sincerity.
explanations.B: This choice converts the developmental history into an obituary the sixth paragraph refutes. The coding never stops, the passage insists; the content turns over while every generation drafts the etiquette its children will be scolded with, so no post-etiquette society appears anywhere.
explanations.C: This choice promotes the fourth paragraph's darker count to the whole indictment's conclusion. Gatekeeping is one function among several, coordination, rehearsal of regard, mutual armor, and the closing question pairs it explicitly with protection, whom the code shields as well as whom it prices out.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage weighs the coordination and small-change accounts against the hypocrisy and gatekeeping charges, gives surface its defense, forms protect the hesitant and restrain the confident, notes the codes' endless turnover, and concludes that manners are a technology of the surface, neither morality nor its opposite, to be judged by whom each code protects and whom it prices out.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: answer the sincerity critique by defending commanded forms as mutual armor protecting the hesitant.

choices.label: B
choices.text: concede that the hypocrisy charge is ultimately unanswerable while shifting the argument's weight onto the separate class critique.

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that offices should restore formal address in order to increase the overall warmth of the workplace.

choices.label: D
choices.text: demonstrate that sincerity cultures produce measurably ruder citizens than politeness cultures do.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph announces the case for surface deserves a fuller hearing, then makes it: warmth cannot be commanded but its forms can, rituals restrain the confident and carve standing for the hesitant, abolished formality admits the advantage of the loudest, and politeness is mutual armor, the alternative to formality being not warmth but exposure. It is the direct rejoinder to the sincerity critique.
explanations.B: This choice concedes what the paragraph contests and reverses the passage's order besides. The class critique precedes this paragraph, and the defense engages the sincerity charge head-on rather than abandoning it as unanswerable.
explanations.C: This choice extracts a policy from an analysis. The office appears as a cautionary illustration of what fills the vacuum when formalities are abolished; no restoration program is proposed, and warmth is precisely what the paragraph says forms cannot command.
explanations.D: This choice invents a comparative measurement the paragraph never conducts. Sincerity cultures are engaged at the level of argument, what their ideal would license, not through any empirical tally of rudeness across societies.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the charge that manners are institutionalized dishonesty is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: full agreement, since the polished cad demonstrates that etiquette exists to disguise cruelty.

choices.label: B
choices.text: acknowledging its force, forms can be performed without content, while rejecting its conclusion, since forms protect where sincerity exposes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: amusement, treating the sincerity cultures' complaint as a misunderstanding with no serious content.

choices.label: D
choices.text: agreement about the etiquette of earlier eras combined with confidence that modern, informal codes have finally eliminated the problem.

explanations.A: This choice mistakes the concession for the verdict. The cad is granted, polished cruelty is crueler for the polish, but the fifth paragraph's defense and the conclusion's both-and formulation, not morality and not its opposite, show the dishonesty charge does not carry the day.
explanations.B: Correct. The third paragraph states the charge at full strength, the performance can even be the perfect disguise, and the passage never denies it; the cad remains standing in the final paragraph as proof that manners are not morality. But the fifth paragraph answers the conclusion drawn from it: a society waiting for genuine feeling extends little courtesy, and the forms protect in the direction sincerity does not.
explanations.C: This choice supplies a dismissiveness the passage's structure contradicts. The charge sheet is called equally old, given a full paragraph, and its central example survives to the conclusion; the engagement is serious throughout, whatever the final balance.
explanations.D: This choice invents a historical improvement the sixth paragraph forecloses. The codes turn over without progressing past the problem; new casualness has its own forks, so no modern code is credited with eliminating the gap between form and feeling.

questions.stem: The description of manners as morality's small change, in the second paragraph, most nearly conveys that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: manners matter less than morality in roughly the way that pocket coins matter less than banknotes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: etiquette rules change frequently while moral principles remain fixed across generations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: everyday courtesies are the minor denominations in which ordinary regard is actually paid out.

choices.label: D
choices.text: politeness can be counterfeited more easily than genuine moral virtue can.

explanations.A: This choice keeps the monetary figure but inverts its point. The metaphor does not rank manners below morality as trivial; it makes them morality's circulating medium, the denominations in which daily regard is transacted, small in unit but constant in use.
explanations.B: This choice hears change as turnover rather than currency. The mutability of codes belongs to the sixth paragraph; here small change is coinage, the everyday tender of consideration, not a claim about how often rules are revised.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph builds the reading directly: table codes as the slow schooling of impulse, each rule a small rehearsal of restraint, civilization's daily practice of considering others, and then the gloss itself, minor denominations, but the currency in which ordinary regard is actually paid. Courtesies are how consideration circulates in daily life.
explanations.D: This choice smuggles the third paragraph's counterfeit problem into the second's metaphor. The small-change figure presents the developmental tradition's positive claim; the possibility of performing forms without content is the critics' later rejoinder, not this phrase's meaning.

questions.stem: A company abolishes titles, dress norms, and meeting protocols to foster authenticity, and within a year junior staff report speaking less in meetings while a few senior voices dominate. Based on the fifth paragraph, this outcome illustrates that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the junior staff lacked the sincerity that the new culture was designed to reward.

choices.label: B
choices.text: meeting protocols were the wrong formality to abolish, though titles and dress norms could have gone safely on their own without harming anyone's standing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: authenticity initiatives fail only in companies whose employees were raised in politeness cultures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: removing the forms released not honesty but exposure: turn-taking rituals had carved standing for the hesitant, and abolition returned it to the loudest.

explanations.A: This choice blames the protected for the failure of the protection. The fifth paragraph's mechanism is structural: the hesitant lose standing when the rituals that carved it vanish, whatever their sincerity, and the loudest gain by default, not by superior authenticity.
explanations.B: This choice invents a taxonomy of safe and unsafe formalities the paragraph never draws. The defense treats forms generally, address, turn-taking, ritual, as armor; nothing licenses sorting titles and dress from protocols as separately abolishable.
explanations.C: This choice adds a cultural precondition found nowhere in the text. The vacuum dynamic, abolish the forms and the advantage of the loudest rushes in, is presented as general, and the paragraph's office example carries no restriction to employees of any upbringing.
explanations.D: Correct. The case is the fifth paragraph's illustration realized: when an office abolishes its formalities in the name of authenticity, what rushes into the vacuum is rarely honesty; it is the advantage of whoever is loudest. The rituals had restrained the confident and carved standing for the hesitant; their removal is exposure, the predicted redistribution toward dominant voices.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the second paragraph's claim that table codes function as a daily rehearsal of considering others?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that the fork was mocked as effeminate for a century after its introduction to European tables.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Studies finding etiquette mastery shows no relationship to considerate behavior outside coded settings, fluent practitioners scoring no higher on regard.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that table codes vary so widely across cultures, eras, and social classes that no single rule of dining is observed everywhere people eat together.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that many people learn table manners in childhood before they can understand the reasons for them.

explanations.A: This choice retells a detail the paragraph itself supplies, the fork as a late and once-ridiculed innovation; the mockery of a code's origin is part of the history and says nothing about whether practicing codes rehearses regard.
explanations.B: Correct. The rehearsal claim is a transfer claim: each rule a small practice of restraint that aggregates into the daily habit of considering others. Fluent practitioners showing no elevated consideration outside coded settings would sever the rehearsal from its supposed product, leaving the forms mastered and the regard unmoved, which is the claim's failure condition.
explanations.C: This choice documents variation in content, which the rehearsal account accommodates: different codes could each school impulse in their own vocabulary, just as different languages each teach speech; universality of particular rules was never the claim.
explanations.D: This choice describes learning before understanding, which the schooling account predicts rather than fears: rehearsal works through repetition, and the second paragraph's slow schooling of impulse requires no early comprehension of reasons.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the fourth paragraph's claim that etiquette fluency functions as a lineage certificate at class borders?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that etiquette manuals have been continuously in print for several centuries across many countries, languages, and successive waves of aspiring readers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surveys finding that most people cannot articulate the rules of etiquette they follow in daily life.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hiring studies finding evaluators rated candidates' polish by unwritten code fluency, those judgments predicting offers independent of competence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that table settings have simplified over the past century, reducing the number of forks in common use.

explanations.A: This choice documents the manual trade the paragraph already notes, and the paragraph's own point about it cuts the other way: manuals sell the passwords, but the passwords are changed, so print longevity shows demand for entry, not the certificate function at the border.
explanations.B: This choice shows the rules operating below articulation, which is consistent with the gate claim but does not test it; inarticulate fluency could serve coordination or habit without ever sorting anyone by class.
explanations.C: Correct. The lineage-certificate claim is that fluency in unwritten codes is read as evidence of origins and used to admit or exclude. Evaluators rating polish and fit by code fluency, with those ratings predicting offers independent of measured competence, capture the certificate doing its gatekeeping work: the code sorting candidates at an actual border, on grounds separate from ability.
explanations.D: This choice tracks the simplification of one code's props, which the paragraph's dynamic absorbs easily, the rules that matter are the unwritten ones, and fewer forks merely relocates the shibboleths; nothing about the border function is tested.
